BackEnd/Databases/Firebase/Basics/Storag.title: AuditingAndFileCounts
BackEnd/Databases/Firebase/Basics/Storag.bodyMd:
# Firebase Storage: Auditing & File Counts --- ### Overview --- Firebase Storage is commonly used to store images, videos, and other files. While the interface looks like it uses folders, Firebase Storage does **not** behave like a traditional file system. This page explains: - why file counts can be confusing - how Firebase Storage actually organizes files - how to correctly count files in nested folders - how to troubleshoot mismatched or unexpected counts The goal is to help you **trust what is actually stored**, even when the UI is misleading. --- ### How Firebase Storage Really Works --- Firebase Storage is built on top of Google Cloud Storage. What looks like folders are actually **path prefixes** inside file names. Example conceptually: - A file may appear inside multiple folders - In reality, it is a single object with a long name Because of this: - folders do not have metadata - folders do not know how many files they contain - counts must be calculated manually This design is efficient for storage systems but confusing for humans. --- ### Limitations of the Firebase Console --- The Firebase Console is useful for basic file management, but it has important limitations. It can: - upload files - download files - delete files - visually browse paths It cannot: - show how many files are inside a folder - count files inside nested folders - detect duplicate files - explain why numbers do not match expectations If you need accurate counts, you must use Google Cloud tools. --- ### Google Cloud Console Overview --- Firebase Storage buckets also appear in the Google Cloud Console. The Cloud Console: - shows the same files as Firebase - provides a stronger UI - still treats folders as prefixes Important limitation: - selecting a folder only counts subfolders - it does not count files inside nested paths The Cloud Console is helpful, but it still cannot give full answers by itself. --- ### Why Command-Line Tools Are Required --- To accurately audit Firebase Storage, you must use command-line tools. Command-line tools can: - list every file recursively - filter by file type - export results for inspection - provide exact counts This works because the CLI operates directly on objects, not folder illusions. --- ### Counting Files Recursively --- The most reliable way to count files is to list all objects recursively and count the results. Generic command structure: ```shell gsutil ls -r gs://BUCKET_NAME/PATH/** | wc -l ``` What this does: - lists every file under a path - includes nested paths - counts each object This produces a true total count. --- ### Counting Only Image Files --- Often you only want to count images. You can filter results by file extension. Generic example: ```shell gsutil ls -r gs://BUCKET_NAME/PATH/** | grep -vi '/$' | grep -Ei '\.(jpg|jpeg|png|webp)$' | wc -l ``` This: - removes folder placeholders - keeps only common image formats - produces a clean image count --- ### Exporting a File List for Review --- When counts do not match expectations, exporting a list of files is very helpful. Generic example: ```shell gsutil ls -r gs://BUCKET_NAME/PATH/** | grep -vi '/$' > storage-files.txt ``` This file can be: - searched - sorted - reviewed manually It is often faster than guessing in the UI. --- ### Creating a Filename-Only List --- Removing folder paths makes rename issues easier to spot. Generic example: ```shell cat storage-files.txt | sed 's#^.*/##' | sort > storage-filenames.txt ``` This helps reveal: - duplicate names - casing differences - extension mismatches --- ### Common Causes of Mismatched Counts --- Unexpected file counts usually come from naming issues, not system errors. Common causes include: - same file uploaded twice - different extensions for the same image - renamed files without deleting the old version - uppercase vs lowercase differences Firebase treats all of these as separate files. --- ### Extension Differences --- These are considered different files: - image.jpg - image.jpeg Even though they look the same, both exist independently. --- ### Case Sensitivity Issues --- Firebase Storage is case-sensitive. These are different files: - CarImage.jpg - carimage.jpg Operating systems may hide this difference, but Firebase does not. --- ### Rename Without Cleanup --- Uploading a renamed file without deleting the old one leaves both in storage. Example scenario: - old-name.jpg remains - new-name.jpg is added Both count toward totals. --- ### Debugging Checklist --- When file counts do not match expectations: 1. Confirm how many files you expect 2. Count files recursively using CLI 3. Filter by file type if needed 4. Export the file list 5. Sort and scan for duplicates 6. Look for extension or casing differences 7. Remove confirmed duplicates 8. Recount to verify This process avoids accidental deletions. --- ### Restoring Missing Files --- If a file is deleted accidentally, recovery depends on where it existed. If the file was tracked in version control: - it can usually be restored from history If the file still exists in Firebase Storage: - it can be downloaded again Command-line tools can copy files back safely. --- ### Safe Renaming Practices --- Firebase Storage does not support true renaming. The safe process is: 1. copy the file to the new name 2. update any references 3. delete the old file Skipping steps can cause broken links or data loss. --- ### Recommended Naming Rules --- To prevent future issues: - use lowercase only - avoid spaces - avoid camelCase - use a single extension consistently - treat filenames as permanent identifiers Consistent naming prevents nearly all auditing problems. --- ### Summary --- Firebase Storage folders are visual conveniences, not real containers. Accurate auditing requires: - understanding how storage works - using command-line tools - verifying results carefully Once you trust the underlying model, file counts become predictable and reliable. ---
← Back
Firebase Storage - Managing Storage Costs
Next →
Section: Firebase Data Modeling & Performance - Modeling Strategies

BackEnd/Databases/MongoDB/Basics/CRUD/Cr.title: Create
BackEnd/Databases/MongoDB/Basics/CRUD/Cr.bodyMd:
# Introduction
Create operations add new documents to a MongoDB collection. If no _id is provided, MongoDB automatically generates an ObjectId.
## insertOne()
Inserts a single document into a collection. insertOne() will only add one item or document to a collection at a time. ```js db.collection('items').insertOne({ text: req.body.text }) ```
  • Returns an acknowledgement and the generated _id.
  • Returns information about the document it creates — including the new document's ID.
  • Fails if a duplicate _id is provided.
The server can send back the newly created document's data using res.json(): ```js res.json({ _id: info.insertedId, text: req.body.text }) ``` This allows the browser to access the new document's ID immediately after creation without a page reload.
## insertMany()
Inserts multiple documents in one operation. ```js db.collectionName.insertMany( [ { key1: 'v1' }, { key1: 'v2', _id: ObjectId() } ], { ordered: true } ); ```
  • Faster than calling insertOne() repeatedly.
  • ordered: true stops on first error (default).
  • ordered: false continues after errors.
  • Missing _id values are auto-generated.
← Back
Section: MongoDB - Basics - Fundamentals - Data Modeling
Next →
MongoDB - Basics - CRUD Operations - Read

BackEnd/Databases/MongoDB/Basics/CRUD/De.title: Delete
BackEnd/Databases/MongoDB/Basics/CRUD/De.bodyMd:
# Introduction
Delete operations remove documents from a collection.
  • Permanently removes data.
  • Can target one or many documents.
  • Uses filter conditions to select documents.
Delete operations are conceptually equivalent to a REST DELETE request.
## deleteOne()
Removes a single matching document from a collection. When using deleteOne() pass only one argument. ```js db.collection('items').deleteOne({ _id: new ObjectId(id) }) ```
## Legacy: .remove()
.remove() is a legacy MongoDB method for deleting documents. It has since been replaced by newer method names and should not be used in modern code.
## Conceptual Notes
  • Deleting a document does not affect other documents.
  • Collections remain even if all documents are removed.
← Back
MongoDB - Basics - CRUD Operations - Update
Next →
MongoDB - Basics - CRUD Operations - Bulk Operations

BackEnd/Databases/MongoDB/Basics/CRUD/Re.title: Read
BackEnd/Databases/MongoDB/Basics/CRUD/Re.bodyMd:
# Introduction
Read operations retrieve documents from a collection. Read is also referred to as load or retrieve — all three terms describe the same concept.
  • Load existing data.
  • Retrieve matching documents.
  • Query based on conditions.
Read operations are conceptually equivalent to a REST GET request.
## find() and toArray()
.find() is the MongoDB way of performing a read operation — the equivalent of load or retrieve in CRUD. ```js db.collection('items').find().toArray() ``` .toArray() converts the result into an easy-to-work-with JavaScript array.
  • If you see commas between items it means JavaScript has auto-converted the array into a string of text.
  • The .join() method can convert an array into a clean string of text when needed.
## Common Read Examples
  • Searching documents in MongoDB Atlas.
  • Filtering collections in MongoDB Compass.
  • Viewing documents via the shell or UI tools.
  • Performing a query search for a specific document based on a certain parameter present in an object.
← Back
MongoDB - Basics - CRUD Operations - Create
Next →
MongoDB - Basics - CRUD Operations - Update

BackEnd/Databases/MongoDB/Basics/CRUD/Up.title: Update
BackEnd/Databases/MongoDB/Basics/CRUD/Up.bodyMd:
# Introduction
Update operations modify existing documents in a collection.
  • Change one or more fields.
  • Leave unspecified fields untouched.
  • Target documents using filter criteria.
### Conceptual Behavior
  • Updates apply to existing documents only.
  • You do not need to resubmit the entire document.
  • Multiple documents can be updated at once.
Update operations are conceptually similar to REST PUT or PATCH requests. Prefer explicit modern APIs such as updateOne and findOneAndUpdate for clarity.
## findOneAndUpdate()
Finds one document in a collection and updates it. ```js db.collection('items').findOneAndUpdate( { _id: new ObjectId(id) }, { $set: { text: newText } } ) ```
  • You only need to provide the fields or properties that are being updated.
  • You do not need to include the full document — only include what is being changed.
## Array Operators
```js { $addToSet: { : } } ``` ```js { $push: { : } } ```
  • $addToSet: adds a value only if it is not already present.
  • $push: appends a value to an array or scalar.
← Back
MongoDB - Basics - CRUD Operations - Read
Next →
MongoDB - Basics - CRUD Operations - Delete

BackEnd/Databases/MongoDB/Basics/Fundame.title: BSONAndObject
BackEnd/Databases/MongoDB/Basics/Fundame.bodyMd:
# BSON & ObjectId
## BSON
MongoDB stores objects in BSON (Binary JSON), structured as key–value pairs and optimized for faster parsing.
  • Similar to JSON but binary and more efficient.
  • Supports embedded documents and arrays.
  • Optimized for parsing performance.
## ObjectId Structure
MongoDB documents use ObjectId as a unique 12-byte identifier. Use _id — not id — when referencing a MongoDB unique identifier.
Component Description
4 bytes Unix timestamp (seconds)
5 bytes Random value generated per process
3 bytes Incrementing counter
## Creating ObjectIds
Shell helper: ```shell ObjectId() ``` In Node.js driver: ```js new ObjectId() ``` ObjectId is specific to MongoDB and its driver package. When using it in Node.js you must import it explicitly: ```js let { MongoClient, ObjectId } = require("mongodb") ```
## Note on Naming
Some older documentation and tools refer to this type as ObjectID(). Modern drivers and the shell typically use ObjectId. They refer to the same identifier type.
← Back
MongoDB - Basics - Fundamentals - Use Cases
Next →
MongoDB - Basics - Fundamentals - Data Modeling

BackEnd/Databases/MongoDB/Basics/Fundame.title: DataModeling
BackEnd/Databases/MongoDB/Basics/Fundame.bodyMd:
# Introduction
MongoDB favors flexible, document-based models over rigid tables. Documents in the same collection can vary in fields and types.
## Collections
  • Collections are conceptually similar to SQL tables but with no enforced schema.
  • Documents can have different keys and types.
  • Related data often lives as embedded objects or arrays rather than separate tables.
## Related Data: Embed vs Reference
  • Embed when data is accessed together frequently — read-optimized, one-to-few.
  • Reference when data is shared broadly, large, or independent.
  • Arrays of embedded documents are common for tightly coupled sub-entities.
## One-to-One Relationships
A one-to-one relationship can be modeled by:
  • Embedding the child subdocument directly.
  • Referencing the child via its ObjectId field — values must match but field names do not need to.
← Back
MongoDB - Basics - Fundamentals - BSON & ObjectId
Next →
Section: MongoDB - Basics - CRUD Operations - Create

BackEnd/Databases/MongoDB/Basics/Fundame.title: Introduction
BackEnd/Databases/MongoDB/Basics/Fundame.bodyMd:
# What Is MongoDB?
  • MongoDB is a NoSQL (non-relational) database management system.
  • Unlike traditional relational databases like PostgreSQL, MongoDB does not store data in tables and rows.
  • Instead it uses documents, objects, and arrays.
## Key Features of MongoDB
  • Open-source and document-oriented.
  • Stores data in collections — groups of documents.
  • Each item that lives inside a collection is called a document.
  • Flexible schemas — documents within a collection can differ in structure.
  • Horizontally scalable and ideal for distributed systems.
  • Commonly used in web development with Node.js and JavaScript.
## BSON (Binary JSON)
  • Stores documents in BSON, a binary-encoded format similar to JSON.
  • Supports embedded documents and arrays.
  • Optimized for parsing performance.
## Scalability and Flexibility
MongoDB is built for scale and rapid change:
  • Stores heterogeneous data with no enforced schema.
  • Supports embedded documents and arrays.
  • Ideal for projects with evolving data models.
## CRUD Overview
CRUD stands for Create, Read, Update, and Delete. These are the four fundamental operations used when working with databases — not just MongoDB, but most if not all databases.
  • Create — adds new documents to a collection.
  • Read — retrieves or loads documents. Also referred to as load or retrieve.
  • Update — edits or modifies an existing document.
  • Delete — removes a document from a collection on the server.
See:
MongoDB → Basics → CRUD Operations → Create
MongoDB → Basics → CRUD Operations → Read
MongoDB → Basics → CRUD Operations → Update
MongoDB → Basics → CRUD Operations → Delete
## Mongoose
Mongoose is a popular Object Data Modeling (ODM) library for MongoDB and Node.js.
  • Adds schemas for structure and validation.
  • Provides cleaner queries and model organization.
  • Analogy: MongoDB is to Mongoose what SQL is to Sequelize.
See: MongoDB → Basics → Mongoose → Overview
← Back
Topic: Firebase - Advanced - Cost Optimization - Storage & Bandwidth
Next →
MongoDB - Basics - Fundamentals - NoSQL vs SQL

BackEnd/Databases/MongoDB/Basics/Fundame.title: NoSQLVsSQL
BackEnd/Databases/MongoDB/Basics/Fundame.bodyMd:
# NoSQL vs SQL --- SQL and NoSQL databases differ in structure and approach:
🔹 SQL → relational, structured tables with fixed schemas 🔹 NoSQL → non-relational, schema-flexible (documents, key-value, graph, etc.) 🔹 SQL excels at strict consistency; NoSQL excels at flexibility and scalability
← Back
MongoDB → Fundamentals → Introduction
Next →
MongoDB → Fundamentals → Setup

BackEnd/Databases/MongoDB/Basics/Fundame.title: Setup
BackEnd/Databases/MongoDB/Basics/Fundame.bodyMd:
# Overview
MongoDB can be run locally or hosted in the cloud using MongoDB Atlas.
## Default Configuration
  • Default MongoDB port: 27017.
  • Databases are created lazily — on first write.
  • Collections do not require predefined schemas.
## MongoDB Atlas & Clusters
When creating a MongoDB account, you typically start by building a cluster. A cluster is where your databases and collections live.
  • Atlas UI may show Build my first cluster or Create Cluster.
  • A single cluster can host multiple databases.
  • Free-tier defaults are sufficient for learning and development.
See: MongoDB → Tools → GUI & CLI → Atlas
## Cloud Providers
When creating a cluster, you choose a cloud provider that physically stores your data.
  • AWS (Amazon Web Services)
  • Google Cloud
  • Microsoft Azure
For most beginner setups, the default region and configuration are acceptable.
## Local vs Cloud
  • Local MongoDB → runs on your machine using mongod.
  • Atlas MongoDB → managed cloud database accessed via connection string.
  • Both expose the same database concepts — databases, collections, and documents.
See:
MongoDB → Tools → Dev Workflow → Connection Strings & Environments
MongoDB → Tools → GUI & CLI → Compass
← Back
MongoDB - Basics - Fundamentals - NoSQL vs SQL
Next →
MongoDB - Basics - Fundamentals - Use Cases

BackEnd/Databases/MongoDB/Basics/Mongoos.title: Introduction
BackEnd/Databases/MongoDB/Basics/Mongoos.bodyMd:
--- ### Mongoose (Overview) --- Mongoose is an Object Data Modeling (ODM) library for MongoDB and Node.js. It sits between your application code and the database, giving you structure, validation, and cleaner APIs. --- ### Why Use Mongoose? ---
🔹 Enforces structure with Schemas (similar to TypeScript enforcing types) 🔹 Provides Models for organized CRUD operations 🔹 Built-in validation (required, min/max, enums, regex) 🔹 Middleware support (hooks like pre-save, post-update) 🔹 Popular in Node.js web apps that need MongoDB
--- ### Analogy --- Think of Mongoose as doing for MongoDB what Sequelize does for SQL:
🔹 MongoDB is flexible and schema-less by default. 🔹 Mongoose adds a thin but powerful layer of structure and safety.
--- ### Where to Learn More --- We’ll explore details in later sections:
🔹 Schemas & Models → defining structure 🔹 Queries & Filters → retrieving data 🔹 Updates & Write Ops → modifying data 🔹 Validation & Middleware → enforcing rules 🔹 Populate & Relationships → linking collections
Next: Schemas & Models

🔹 Filters results by the given condition(s) 🔹 Roughly analogous to a REST GET

BackEnd/Databases/MongoDB/Basics/Mongoos.title: SchemasAndModels
BackEnd/Databases/MongoDB/Basics/Mongoos.bodyMd:
# Schemas & Models --- Define a Model (class) tied to a collection using a Schema. ```js mongoose.model("ModelName", schema); ```
🔹 Compiles a Schema into a Model bound to a collection 🔹 Models expose APIs to create, query, update, delete
--- ### Creating Documents --- Persist new data using Mongoose models. Model.create is a one-shot helper; new Model + save lets you prepare or mutate before saving. ```js // One-shot create (validates + writes) await Model.create({ name: 'A' }); // Two-step creation with extra logic before persisting const doc = new Model({ name: 'B' }); // ... mutate doc, run custom logic, attach refs, etc. await doc.save(); ```
🔹 Both paths run schema validators and relevant middleware (hooks) 🔹 create also accepts arrays for batch inserts 🔹 save is ideal when you need pre-save transformations or conditional logic
← Back
MongoDB → Mongoose → Introduction
Next →
MongoDB → Mongoose → Queries & Filters

BackEnd/Databases/MongoDB/Basics/Mongoos.title: Updates
BackEnd/Databases/MongoDB/Basics/Mongoos.bodyMd:
--- ### Updates & Write Ops --- Modify existing documents. ```js update(criteria, updateData); ```
🔹 Equivalent to a REST PUT at a high level 🔹 Prefer explicit modern APIs (e.g., updateOne, findByIdAndUpdate) for clarity
--- ### Array Operators (Basics) --- ```js { $addToSet: { : , ... } } ``` ```js { $push: { : , ... } } ```
🔹 $addToSet: add value if not already present 🔹 $push: append value (array or scalar)
--- ### Deletes --- Remove documents from a collection using model helpers. ```js Model.deleteOne({ field: value }) Model.deleteMany({ field: value }) Model.findByIdAndDelete(id) ```
🔹 deleteOne → remove a single matching document 🔹 deleteMany → remove all matching documents 🔹 findByIdAndDelete → remove by ObjectId shortcut 🔹 Equivalent to a REST DELETE request
← Back
Queries & Filters
Next →
Validation & Middleware

BackEnd/Databases/MongoDB/Tools/DevWorkf.title: ConnectionStrings
BackEnd/Databases/MongoDB/Tools/DevWorkf.bodyMd:
# What a Connection String Does
A connection string is used by tools and applications to connect to a MongoDB database. It tells the client:
  • Where the database is hosted.
  • How to authenticate.
  • Which database to connect to.
## Local vs Atlas Environments
  • Local MongoDB commonly uses mongodb://.
  • MongoDB Atlas commonly uses mongodb+srv://.
  • Both formats represent the same concept — how to reach and access the database.
## Database Users & Credentials
When using MongoDB Atlas, you must create a database user with:
  • A username.
  • A password.
These credentials are required to authenticate and are referenced by the connection process.
## Editing an Atlas Connection String
Atlas provides connection options such as:
  • Connect Your Application
  • A section labeled something like Connection String Only
Common edits include:
  • Replacing <password> with your actual password.
  • Replacing test with the database name you want to use.
  • The database name can be found or created in the Collections section of Atlas.
## Atlas Network Access
To allow your application or tools to connect to an Atlas cluster, network access may need to be configured. A common development option is adding 0.0.0.0/0 which allows connections from any IP address. This is convenient for development but not recommended for production.
← Back
MongoDB - Tools - Dev Workflow - Seeding & Local Dev
Next →
Topic: MySQL - Basics - Fundamentals - Introduction

BackEnd/Databases/MongoDB/Tools/DevWorkf.title: SeedingAndLocal
BackEnd/Databases/MongoDB/Tools/DevWorkf.bodyMd:
# Seeding & Local Dev
## What Is Seeding?
Seeding is the process of inserting starter or test data into a database so you can develop and test features without manually creating documents every time.
## Running a Seed Script
Run your project's seed script if defined in your project configuration: ```shell npm run seed ```
  • Useful for local development and testing.
  • Common after wiping or resetting your database.
  • Depends on your project having a seed script set up.
← Back

BackEnd/Databases/MongoDB/Tools/GUIAndCL.title: Atlas
BackEnd/Databases/MongoDB/Tools/GUIAndCL.bodyMd:
# MongoDB Atlas
## What Is MongoDB Atlas?
MongoDB Atlas is a fully managed, cloud-hosted MongoDB service. It allows you to run MongoDB without managing servers or infrastructure.
## Clusters
In Atlas, databases are hosted inside clusters.
  • A cluster can contain multiple databases.
  • Cluster creation is typically the first step when setting up Atlas.
  • Free-tier clusters are sufficient for learning and development.
## Cloud Providers
Atlas allows you to choose where your data is hosted.
  • Amazon Web Services (AWS)
  • Google Cloud
  • Microsoft Azure
The default region and configuration are usually adequate for getting started.
## Users & Network Access
Atlas requires configuration for access and security.
  • Database users define authentication credentials.
  • Network access controls which IPs can connect.
  • Development setups often relax restrictions for convenience.
## Connection Strings
Atlas provides connection strings for connecting tools and applications to your cluster.
  • Used by Compass and application code.
  • Include host, authentication, and database information.
See: MongoDB → Tools → Dev Workflow → Connection Strings & Environments
## Atlas CLI
MongoDB also provides a command-line interface for interacting with Atlas.
  • Used for managing Atlas resources from the terminal.
  • Complements the Atlas web UI.
← Back
MongoDB - Tools - GUI & CLI - Compass
Next →
Section: MongoDB - Tools - Data Move & Backup - Export & Import

BackEnd/Databases/MongoDB/Tools/GUIAndCL.title: Compass
BackEnd/Databases/MongoDB/Tools/GUIAndCL.bodyMd:
# MongoDB Compass
## What Is Compass?
MongoDB Compass is a graphical interface for working with MongoDB databases.
  • View and edit documents.
  • Run queries visually.
  • Explore collections and indexes.
  • Inspect schema structure.
## Connecting to a Database
Compass connects to MongoDB using a connection string.
  • Can connect to a local MongoDB instance.
  • Can connect to a MongoDB Atlas cluster.
  • The connection string defines host, authentication, and database.
See: MongoDB → Tools → Dev Workflow → Connection Strings & Environments
## Relationship to Other Tools
  • Compass provides a visual alternative to shell-based interaction.
  • It is commonly used alongside Atlas and local development environments.

BackEnd/Databases/MongoDB/Tools/ShellAnd.title: Commands
BackEnd/Databases/MongoDB/Tools/ShellAnd.bodyMd:
--- ### Shell & Server Commands --- Key commands for controlling the MongoDB server and interacting with it via the Mongo Shell. --- ### Starting the Server (mongod) --- ```shell mongod ```
🔹 Starts the server daemon that stores data and accepts connections 🔹 Defaults: port 27017, data path /data/db 🔹 Use --dbpath for a custom data directory; --port for a non-default port 🔹 Dev: run in a terminal; Prod: run under a service manager
Stop the server (Linux/macOS): ```shell pkill mongod ```
🔹 Sends a signal to stop all mongod processes (Unix/macOS) 🔹 On Windows/managed services, stop via the OS/service tooling
--- ### Starting the Shell (mongosh) --- ```shell mongosh ``` #### Connect to a specific database: ```shell mongosh "mongodb://localhost:27017/appDatabase" ```
🔹 Opens the interactive shell to run admin commands and queries 🔹 Without a URI, connects to local defaults; with a URI, targets a specific host/db 🔹 Use SRV (mongodb+srv://) for Atlas, or mongodb:// for local/standalone
--- ### Getting Help --- ```shell help ``` ```shell db.help() ```
🔹 help shows global shell help; db.help() lists database-scoped helpers 🔹 Many objects also support .help() to discover methods
--- ### Legacy UI tip (historical) --- In older MongoDB shell UIs you may see an “i” (info) icon listing keyboard shortcuts and helpful commands. In modern mongosh, use: help and db.help(). --- ### Database Navigation --- ```shell show dbs use myDatabase db show collections ```
🔹 show dbs lists databases 🔹 use switches the active db (created lazily on first write) 🔹 db prints the current db; show collections lists collections in the active db
--- ### Admin & Diagnostics --- ```js db.stats() db.serverStatus() db.currentOp() ```
🔹 db.stats() → summary of the current database 🔹 db.serverStatus() → server health overview 🔹 db.currentOp() → view running operations
--- ### Replica Set Startup (Preview) --- ```shell mongod --replSet "rs0" --dbpath /data/db/rs0 --port 27017 --bind_ip_all ``` ```js rs.initiate() rs.status() ```
🔹 --replSet starts a node intended for replica set membership 🔹 rs.initiate() bootstraps a new replica set; rs.status() inspects its state 🔹 For production, follow full replication/sharding best practices
← Back

BackEnd/Databases/MongoDB/Tools/ShellAnd.title: Introduction
BackEnd/Databases/MongoDB/Tools/ShellAnd.bodyMd:

🔹 mongod → the **server daemon** that stores data and accepts connections 🔹 mongosh → the **interactive shell** for connecting, querying, and administering databases
--- ### Typical Workflow --- 1. Start the server with mongod 2. Connect using mongosh 3. Run commands to inspect or manage your environment --- ### Quick Example --- ```shell mongod --dbpath /data/db ``` ```shell mongosh "mongodb://localhost:27017" ``` --- ### Where to Go Next ---

BackEnd/Databases/PostgreSQL/Basics/Core.title: SchemasTables
BackEnd/Databases/PostgreSQL/Basics/Core.bodyMd:
# Schemas & Tables --- ### Overview --- PostgreSQL organizes data using schemas and tables. Schemas help group related database objects, while tables store structured rows of data. --- ### Creating a Table Tables define the structure of stored data, including columns and data types. Example: ```sql CREATE TABLE your_table_name ( id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY, column1 TEXT, column2 TEXT ); ```
🔹 This structure can be modified based on your data needs 🔹 SERIAL creates an auto-incrementing integer column (commonly used for primary keys)
--- ### Column Types Common column types include: - TEXT - INTEGER - BOOLEAN - TIMESTAMP PostgreSQL supports many additional data types depending on use case. --- ### Primary Keys Primary keys uniquely identify rows in a table. In PostgreSQL, SERIAL is commonly used to generate auto-incrementing identifiers. --- ### Summary Schemas and tables form the foundation of PostgreSQL data storage and structure.
← Back
Setup & Configuration

CRUD (SQL Statements)

BackEnd/Databases/PostgreSQL/Basics/Fund.bodyMd:
# Features & Use Cases --- ### Feature Highlights ---
🔹 Strong SQL support (including advanced query features) 🔹 Extensible via extensions 🔹 Supports transactions and concurrency features (covered later under Transactions & Locking) 🔹 Supports many database features commonly needed in production systems (indexes, views, etc.)
--- ### Common Use Cases ---
🔹 Web applications needing a relational database 🔹 Systems that require data integrity and structured relationships 🔹 Reporting and analytics use cases using SQL queries 🔹 Projects that benefit from database-side features (views, indexing strategies, extensions)
--- ### Where These Topics Live In This Notes Set ---
🔹 Indexes → Intermediate → Indexes & Performance 🔹 Transactions → Intermediate → Transactions & Locking 🔹 Advanced SQL features → Advanced → Advanced SQL 🔹 Extensions and external data → Advanced → Extensions & FDW

BackEnd/Databases/PostgreSQL/Basics/Fund.title: Introduction
BackEnd/Databases/PostgreSQL/Basics/Fund.bodyMd:
# Introduction --- ### What PostgreSQL Is --- PostgreSQL (Postgres) is an open-source relational database management system (RDBMS) that uses and extends SQL.
🔹 Open-source and widely used 🔹 Relational database (tables, rows, schemas) 🔹 Known for reliability and feature robustness
--- ### Quick Sanity Check (SQL) --- After you’re connected to a database, you can run a simple query to confirm everything is working: sql SELECT version();
🔹 Returns the PostgreSQL server version string 🔹 Useful to confirm you are connected and can run SQL
## PostgreSQL Introduction --- ### What PostgreSQL Is Used For --- PostgreSQL is commonly used for: - Relational data storage - Backend databases for web applications - Analytical and reporting workloads - Systems requiring data integrity and consistency --- ### Key Characteristics - **Open-source** PostgreSQL is community-driven and freely available. - **SQL-compliant** It supports standard SQL while offering advanced features. - **Extensible** Users can define custom data types, functions, and extensions. - **Scalable** Suitable for both small applications and large production systems. --- ### Summary PostgreSQL is a powerful relational database designed for correctness, flexibility, and long-term maintainability.

BackEnd/Databases/PostgreSQL/Basics/Setu.title: Installing
BackEnd/Databases/PostgreSQL/Basics/Setu.bodyMd:
# Installing PostgreSQL --- ### Overview Before using PostgreSQL, it must be installed and initialized on the system. Installation methods vary by operating system, but once installed, PostgreSQL provides both command-line and graphical tools for managing databases. --- ### Creating a Database (After Install) --- Once PostgreSQL is installed and you can access psql, you can create a database: ```sql CREATE DATABASE your_database_name; ``` ### After Installation Once PostgreSQL is installed: - The PostgreSQL service is running - A default administrative user is created - Client tools such as psql are available The default administrative user is typically: postgres --- ### Next Steps After installation, you will typically connect to PostgreSQL using a client tool. For command-line usage and basic administration, see: psql Basics --- ### Summary Installing PostgreSQL prepares the system for database creation and management using built-in tools and clients.

GraphQLAndApollo/Apollo/ApolloClient/Bas.bodyMd:
## Apollo Client Cache Store --- ### Overview Apollo Client includes a caching layer that can store previously fetched GraphQL results. Caching helps improve performance and reduce repeated network requests. --- ### InMemoryCache (Concept) InMemoryCache is the default cache implementation. It stores GraphQL results in memory so previously fetched data can be returned quickly without refetching.

GraphQLAndApollo/Apollo/ApolloClient/Bas.title: InstallAndSetup
GraphQLAndApollo/Apollo/ApolloClient/Bas.bodyMd:
## Apollo Client Install and Setup

---

### Overview

Apollo Client is typically configured once near the root of an application.

This is where the client instance and cache are created, and the app is wrapped so components can access GraphQL data.

---

### ApolloProvider (Concept)

ApolloProvider makes the Apollo Client available to the entire application.

This enables consistent access to queries, mutations, and cache features throughout the UI.

GraphQLAndApollo/Apollo/ApolloClient/Bas.title: Introduction
GraphQLAndApollo/Apollo/ApolloClient/Bas.bodyMd:
## Apollo Client Introduction --- ### Overview Apollo Client is a client-side library for fetching and managing GraphQL data in an application. It commonly provides: - Query and mutation helpers - Built-in caching - Integration patterns for UI frameworks like React --- ### Apollo Client as State Management Apollo Client often acts as a data layer that can: - Fetch GraphQL data - Cache results for fast reuse - Keep UI in sync with server data
← Back
GraphQL
Next →
Install & Setup

GraphQLAndApollo/Apollo/ApolloClient/Bas.title: UseMutations
GraphQLAndApollo/Apollo/ApolloClient/Bas.bodyMd:
## useMutation

---

### Overview

useMutation is used to send GraphQL mutations from a client application.

Mutations are commonly used to change data on the server (create, update, delete actions).

---

### refetchQueries (Concept)

refetchQueries is an option that allows specified queries to be refetched after a mutation completes.

This is a common way to ensure UI data stays up-to-date after changes.

GraphQLAndApollo/Apollo/ApolloServer/Adv.title: ResponseCache
GraphQLAndApollo/Apollo/ApolloServer/Adv.bodyMd:
## Response Cache (Performance) --- ### Overview Caching is a common way to improve performance in GraphQL APIs. Caching can reduce repeated work for identical or similar requests. --- ### Query Caching (High-Level) Caching strategies typically focus on: - Reducing repeated resolver/database work - Returning cached results for repeated operations - Controlling cache lifetime to avoid stale data --- ### Query Batching (High-Level) Batching groups multiple requests or operations together to reduce overhead. This can improve efficiency when a client triggers many related requests. --- ### Monitoring and Logging (Performance Context) Performance improvements are usually paired with: - Monitoring slow operations - Logging failures and latency trends - Identifying high-cost resolvers and frequent queries
← Back
Cache Hints
Next →
Observability

GraphQLAndApollo/Apollo/ApolloServer/Adv.title: ErrorMasks
GraphQLAndApollo/Apollo/ApolloServer/Adv.bodyMd:
## Error Masks and Safer Errors --- ### Overview GraphQL errors should be useful to clients while avoiding leaking sensitive internal details. Error masking is the practice of controlling what the client sees versus what is logged internally. --- ### Logging Errors (High-Level) Recommended goals: - Log detailed errors internally for debugging - Return safe, consistent errors to the client - Avoid leaking stack traces, secrets, or internal implementation details --- ### Handling GraphQL Validation Errors (High-Level) Validation errors occur when a request does not match the schema. Common approaches include: - Returning a clear client-facing error message - Logging the operation and relevant metadata for investigation
← Back
Plugin API
Next →
Cache & Hints

GraphQLAndApollo/Apollo/ApolloServer/Adv.title: CheckAndContracts
GraphQLAndApollo/Apollo/ApolloServer/Adv.bodyMd:
## Schema Governance (Checks and Contracts)

---

### Overview

Schema governance is about keeping a GraphQL schema safe, stable, and maintainable as it evolves.

This often includes validation, compatibility checks, and coordination across teams.

---

### Apollo Federation (High-Level)

Federation is an approach to composing a larger graph from multiple services.

This topic is often related to governance because it impacts:

- ownership boundaries
- schema composition
- change management

---

### Schema Stitching (High-Level)

Schema stitching is another approach for composing schemas.

It can be useful in certain situations but typically introduces complexity that governance processes need to manage.

---

### Notes

This page is a placeholder for deeper governance workflows and tooling details.

GraphQLAndApollo/Apollo/ApolloServer/Adv.title: WebSocketGraphQLWs
GraphQLAndApollo/Apollo/ApolloServer/Adv.bodyMd:
## Subscriptions Transport (WebSocket)

---

### Overview

Subscriptions enable real-time updates in GraphQL.

Instead of request/response like queries and mutations, subscriptions keep a connection open so the server can push updates when events occur.

---

### Real-Time Updates with Subscriptions

Common subscription use cases:

- Live notifications
- Chat messages
- Dashboard updates
- Collaboration events

---

### Notes

This page focuses on the transport conceptually.

Implementation details can be expanded later when you add concrete examples and recommended libraries.

GraphQLAndApollo/Apollo/ApolloServer/Bas.title: Introduction
GraphQLAndApollo/Apollo/ApolloServer/Bas.bodyMd:
## Apollo Server (Introduction) --- ### Overview Apollo Server is a community-driven, open-source, spec-compliant GraphQL server used to build GraphQL APIs. It provides the backend layer responsible for defining a GraphQL schema and resolving client requests against underlying data sources. ---
🔹 Connects GraphQL schemas to data sources 🔹 Works with Node.js and popular frameworks (Express, Koa, etc.) 🔹 Handles queries, mutations, and subscriptions 🔹 Integrates well with Apollo Client
--- ### What Apollo Server Helps With Apollo Server focuses on the **server-side GraphQL API**, including: - Exposing a GraphQL endpoint - Defining the schema that describes available data and operations - Implementing resolvers that fetch or compute requested fields - Integrating with databases, REST APIs, and other backend services - Structuring a GraphQL API in a scalable and maintainable way --- ### Related Topics Apollo Server handles the backend GraphQL layer. Client-side concerns such as caching, UI integration, and hooks (for example Apollo Client, InMemoryCache, and React hooks) are covered in the Apollo Client section.
← Back
Tooling
Next →
Install & Setup

GraphQLAndApollo/Apollo/ApolloServer/Bas.title: Setup
GraphQLAndApollo/Apollo/ApolloServer/Bas.bodyMd:
## Apollo Server Setup --- ### Overview --- Setting up Apollo Server involves installing the required packages, defining a GraphQL schema, connecting that schema to application logic, and starting a server that exposes a GraphQL endpoint. This page focuses on the high-level setup flow rather than detailed implementation. --- ### Basic Setup Flow ---
🔹 Install apollo-server and graphql 🔹 Define a schema with typeDefs 🔹 Provide resolvers to connect schema to logic/data 🔹 Start the server and test queries in Apollo Sandbox or Playground
--- ### What Happens During Setup --- At a high level, Apollo Server setup establishes the core pieces required to run a GraphQL API.
🔹 A GraphQL schema that defines available data and operations 🔹 Resolver functions that fetch or compute requested fields 🔹 A running server process that listens for GraphQL operations
Once these pieces are in place, the server is ready to handle queries, mutations, and subscriptions. --- ### Server Environment Options --- Apollo Server can be deployed in multiple environments depending on application needs.
🔹 Standalone Node.js servers 🔹 HTTP framework integrations (such as Express or Koa) 🔹 Serverless or edge environments
The overall setup flow remains conceptually similar, even though implementation details may vary by environment. --- ### Next Steps --- After completing basic setup, developers typically move on to deeper server configuration and design.
🔹 Schema design and modularization 🔹 Resolver implementation patterns 🔹 Context and request lifecycle handling 🔹 Performance, caching, and observability features
These topics are covered in the corresponding Apollo Server sections.

GraphQLAndApollo/Apollo/ApolloServer/Bas.title: Resolvers
GraphQLAndApollo/Apollo/ApolloServer/Bas.bodyMd:
## Apollo Server Resolvers

---

### Overview

Resolvers are functions that provide the actual data for fields defined in the schema.

When a client requests fields, the server runs the resolver logic to fetch or compute the values.

---

### Debugging Resolvers (High-Level)

Resolver debugging usually focuses on:

- Confirming the resolver is being called
- Verifying inputs and returned data
- Identifying failed upstream calls (database, REST services)
- Handling unexpected null or missing fields

---

### Logging Errors (High-Level)

Common resolver troubleshooting patterns include:

- Logging errors with enough context to diagnose the failing operation
- Returning safe errors to clients while keeping internal details private

GraphQLAndApollo/Apollo/ApolloServer/Bas.title: Schema
GraphQLAndApollo/Apollo/ApolloServer/Bas.bodyMd:
## Apollo Server Schema

---

### Overview

A GraphQL schema describes the shape of your API.

It defines what data can be queried, what mutations can be performed, and which types exist in the API.

---

### Schema Definition and Modularization

As an API grows, the schema is commonly split into smaller pieces to keep it organized.

Modularizing a schema helps with:

- Maintaining readability
- Team ownership of parts of the schema
- Scaling the API over time

---

### Declarative Data Fetching (High-Level)

Clients request exactly the fields they need from the schema.

The schema defines what is possible to request, while resolvers define how the data is actually retrieved.

# Components --- Angular components are the **building blocks** of Angular applications: they combine a class, template, and metadata to define UI behavior. --- ## Anatomy of a Component --- An Angular component consists of: - A TypeScript class handling data and logic - A template defining the UI - Metadata via @Component decorator Example: ```ts import { Component } from '@angular/core' @Component({ selector: 'app-hello', template: `

{{title}}

` }) export class HelloComponent { title = 'Hello, Angular!' update() { this.title = 'Title Changed!' } } ``` The (click) syntax binds click events to update()—a central part of component interactivity. --- ## Why Components Matter --- - Promote **reusability** and encapsulation - Support structured UI composition - Enable data and event binding between parent and child components Angular uses components to build scalable, maintainable applications. > Next: Learn about reusable design patterns and how to structure components.

FrontEnd/Angular/Basics/CoreConcepts/Mod.bodyMd:
# Angular Modules – Basics An Angular module (NgModule) groups components, directives, pipes, and services into cohesive units. --- ## What Is an NgModule? The @NgModule decorator organizes application code: ```ts import { NgModule } from '@angular/core' import { BrowserModule } from '@angular/platform-browser' import { AppComponent } from './app.component' import { SharedComponent } from './shared/shared.component' @NgModule({ declarations: [AppComponent, SharedComponent], imports: [BrowserModule], providers: [], bootstrap: [AppComponent] }) export class AppModule {} ``` - declarations: declares components and pipes - imports: brings in dependencies - providers: available services - bootstrap: root component that launches the app --- ## Why Use Modules - Improve **code organization** and separation - Enable **lazy loading** of features - Prepare Angular for optimized builds (AOT) NgModules form the structural backbone of your application.

# Angular Routing – Basics Routing lets you map URLs to components, enabling navigation without full-page reloads. --- ## 🔗 Basic Route Configuration ```ts import { NgModule } from '@angular/core' import { RouterModule, Routes } from '@angular/router' import { HomeComponent } from './home.component' import { AboutComponent } from './about.component' const routes: Routes = [ { path: '', component: HomeComponent }, { path: 'about', component: AboutComponent } ] @NgModule({ imports: [RouterModule.forRoot(routes)], exports: [RouterModule] }) export class AppRoutingModule {} ``` Use RouterModule.forRoot(routes) to register routes at the app root. --- ## 🧭 Router Outlet & Links Place <router-outlet> in your AppComponent template: ```html
Home | About
``` routerLink updates the URL and displays the associated component. > Next: Learn about guards, resolvers, and protected routes.

# Angular Services – Basics Angular services are singleton objects that encapsulate shared logic and support Dependency Injection (DI). --- ## Creating a Service ```ts import { Injectable } from '@angular/core' @Injectable({ providedIn: 'root' }) export class DataService { private items: string[] = ['One', 'Two', 'Three'] getItems(): string[] { return this.items } addItem(item: string): void { this.items.push(item) } } ``` The @Injectable({ providedIn: 'root' }) decorator makes the service available app-wide—no need to add it to `providers`. --- ## Injecting into Components ```ts @Component({...}) export class AppComponent { items: string[] constructor(private data: DataService) { this.items = data.getItems() } } ``` Dependency Injection ensures you use a single shared instance of DataService.

FrontEnd/React/Basics/CoreConcepts/Forms.title: Forms
FrontEnd/React/Basics/CoreConcepts/Forms.bodyMd:
# Forms & Inputs --- ## Handling Form Inputs in React React uses a controlled component pattern to manage form elements like <input>, <textarea>, and <select>. This means the form data is handled by React state. --- ## Controlled Components A controlled component is an input element whose value is controlled by React state. ### Example: Text Input ```javascript import { useState } from 'react'; function NameForm() { const [name, setName] = useState(''); return (
); } ``` - value is bound to React state. - onChange updates the state when the user types. --- ## Why Use Controlled Components? - Easier to validate input data - Lets you disable/enable buttons based on input - Enables dynamic behavior (e.g. live character count) --- ## Handling Checkboxes Checkboxes use a boolean value tied to state. ```javascript const [isChecked, setIsChecked] = useState(false); setIsChecked(e.target.checked)} /> ``` --- ## Handling Select Menus Select elements follow the same pattern: ```javascript const [fruit, setFruit] = useState('apple'); ``` --- ## Form Submission To handle form submission: ```javascript function handleSubmit(e) { e.preventDefault(); console.log('Form submitted!'); }
...
``` - Prevents default page refresh behavior. - Useful for sending data to an API or validating before submission. --- --- ## Full Form Example with Multiple Inputs This example demonstrates handling multiple input types within a single form: ```jsx function FormExample() { const [text, setText] = useState("") const [bio, setBio] = useState("") const [color, setColor] = useState("#00bfff") const [volume, setVolume] = useState(50) const [agreed, setAgreed] = useState(false) return (
{ e.preventDefault() alert("Form submitted!") }}>

FrontEnd/React/Basics/CoreConcepts/Props.title: Props
FrontEnd/React/Basics/CoreConcepts/Props.bodyMd:
--- ## What Are Props? --- Props are short for “properties”, allow a component to receive data from its parent. They make components customizable and dynamic by enabling different data to be passed at render time.
- Props are read-only. A child component cannot modify the prop values it receives. - You can pass strings, numbers, arrays, objects, functions even JSX as props.
--- ## Example: Customizing a Component with Props --- Here’s a functional component named Pet that takes props and displays them in a list item. ### JSX Example ```javascript function Pet(props) { return (
  • {props.name} is a {props.species} and is {props.age} years old.
    • ); } ``` --- ### How It Works ---
    - props.name: Displays the pet’s name. - props.species: Indicates the type of animal. - props.age: Displays the pet’s age.
    This structure makes the Pet component reusable. You can render it multiple times with different props: ```javascript ``` --- ## Best Practices for Props --- - Use propTypes or TypeScript interfaces to validate expected props (if applicable). - Provide default values with defaultProps or default parameters. - Avoid unnecessary prop nesting — pass only what the child component actually needs. - Destructure props inside the component for cleaner code: ```javascript function Pet({ name, species, age }) { return
  • {name} is a {species} and is {age} years old.
    • ; } ``` --- ## Summary --- - Props are passed from parent to child components - They are immutable in the receiving component - Make components reusable and configurable - Props can be strings, numbers, arrays, objects, or functions
    ← Back
    Core Concepts
    Next →
    State

FrontEnd/React/Basics/CoreConcepts/Props.title: State
FrontEnd/React/Basics/CoreConcepts/Props.bodyMd:
--- ## What Is State? State is a built-in feature in React that allows components to store dynamic data that can change over time. When the state changes, the component re-renders to reflect the new data. - Think of state as the “memory” of a component. - It is local by default and scoped to the component in which it's defined. --- ## What Are Hooks? Hooks are functions that let you "hook into" React features from functional components. Before hooks, most dynamic behavior relied on class components. Now, hooks allow functional components to manage state, lifecycle, and side effects. - Hooks work only in functional components (not classes) - They follow strict rules to ensure consistent behavior - They allow reusable logic via custom hooks --- ## Core Rules of Hooks - Only call hooks at the top level — never inside loops, conditions, or nested functions. - Only call hooks from React functions — either your component or a custom hook. These rules ensure consistent state handling and predictable behavior. --- ## Common Built-in Hooks
Hook Purpose
useState Adds local component state
useEffect Runs side effects after rendering
useContext Accesses context values
useReducer Manages complex state logic
useRef References DOM elements or persistent values
useMemo Optimizes expensive calculations
useCallback Memoizes callback functions
--- ## Managing State with useState useState is a React Hook that adds local state to functional components. ### Example: Basic Counter ```javascript import { useState } from 'react'; function Counter() { const [count, setCount] = useState(0); return (

You clicked {count} times

); } ``` - count is the current state value. - setCount is the function that updates count. - useState(0) sets the initial value to 0. --- ## When to Use State Use state when a component needs to: - Respond to user input - Track dynamic values (like counters, forms, toggles) - Handle component-specific behavior that changes over time --- ## useReducer for Complex State useReducer is an alternative to useState for managing more complex state logic. - Ideal when the next state depends on the previous one - Works well when handling multiple related values ### Example: Basic useReducer Skeleton ```javascript const initialState = { count: 0 }; function reducer(state, action) { switch (action.type) { case 'increment': return { count: state.count + 1 }; default: return state; } } const [state, dispatch] = useReducer(reducer, initialState); ``` --- ## Global State with Context To share state across many components without prop drilling, use the Context API: - Combine useContext with useState or useReducer - Common use cases: authentication, themes, user preferences --- ## Hook Naming Convention React relies on naming to detect hooks. All custom hook functions must start with use — like useAuth or useFormData. If you don't follow this naming pattern, React can't verify that the rules of hooks are being followed. --- ## Summary - State stores dynamic values inside components - useState handles local, simple values - useReducer is better for complex logic - useContext enables shared/global state - Hooks enable reactivity in functional components - Changing state causes re-rendering

Testing/EndToEnd/Cypress/Basics/CoreComm.title: VisitGet
Testing/EndToEnd/Cypress/Basics/CoreComm.bodyMd:
## Visit and Get --- These two commands form the **core building blocks** of Cypress test interactions: - cy.visit() loads a URL into the test browser - cy.get() selects a DOM element using a CSS selector They’re almost always the first commands in any test block. --- ## cy.visit() This command navigates the Cypress browser to a given URL. ```javascript cy.visit('/dashboard') ``` ✅ Automatically waits for the page to finish loading ✅ Works with relative or absolute URLs You’ll usually place cy.visit() inside a beforeEach() or at the top of your it() block. ### Example: ```javascript cy.visit('/') cy.contains('Welcome').should('exist') ``` --- ## cy.get() This command selects an element on the page using a **CSS selector**. ```javascript cy.get('.form-input') ``` ✅ Automatically retries for a default timeout ✅ Works well with .should(), .click(), .type(), etc. ✅ Accepts any valid CSS selector ### Example: ```javascript cy.get('#username').type('admin') cy.get('button[type="submit"]').click() ``` --- ## Best Practices ✅ Prefer **IDs** or **data-* attributes** over dynamic class names ✅ Avoid selectors tied to styling frameworks (e.g. Tailwind or Bootstrap classes) ✅ Use consistent data-cy or data-testid attributes for stability ```javascript cy.get('[data-cy="submit-button"]').click() ``` ✅ Alias selectors for readability and reuse: ```javascript cy.get('.menu-item').as('menu') cy.get('@menu').click() ``` --- ## cy.visit() vs. cy.request() cy.visit() opens the browser like a real user. cy.request() performs HTTP requests in the background without rendering the UI.
Feature cy.visit() cy.request()
Loads full UI ✅ Yes ❌ No
Test interactions with elements ✅ Yes ❌ No
Mock or test API endpoints ❌ No ✅ Yes
--- ## Summary ✅ cy.visit() = Go to this place ✅ cy.get() = Find this thing These are the foundation of almost every Cypress test. Use them confidently before chaining assertions and actions.
← Back
Syntax
Next →
cy.contains() & cy.find()

Testing/EndToEnd/Cypress/Basics/Fundamen.title: Introduction
Testing/EndToEnd/Cypress/Basics/Fundamen.bodyMd:
--- ## What Is Cypress? --- Cypress is a modern, end-to-end testing framework built for the modern web. 🔹 It runs directly in the browser alongside your application 🔹 Designed for JavaScript/TypeScript developers 🔹 Ideal for testing modern front-end frameworks like React, Vue, Angular --- ## Why Use Cypress? 🔹 Fast feedback loop with hot reloading of tests 🔹 Runs in the same run-loop as the app — gives better visibility 🔹 Uses a real browser — no WebDriver layer required 🔹 Built-in wait, retry, and assertion handling 🔹 Friendly debugging with time-travel snapshots and error messages 🔹 Excellent integration with CI tools like GitHub Actions and CircleCI --- ## Key Features at a Glance 🔹 Easy to install with npm 🔹 Visual test runner or headless CLI support 🔹 Built-in network traffic control (intercept, mock) 🔹 Easy to write readable tests using chaining 🔹 Compatible with component and integration testing --- ## Cypress Mental Model Tests in Cypress are written in a way that feels like **instructions in a sentence**. Each test block reads like a simple command:
Cypress Command Analogy
cy.visit() Go to this place
cy.get() Find this thing
cy.contains() Look for this text
cy.click() Click this
cy.should() Verify it behaves like this
--- ## Cypress Test Structure Analogy 🧠 Think of a Cypress file like a **scripted sentence**: - describe() = "This is the topic we're testing" - it() = "This specific thing should happen" - Inside each it(): - cy.visit() = "Go to this place" - cy.get() = "Find this thing" - cy.contains() = "Look for this text" - cy.click() = "Click this" - cy.should() = "Verify it behaves like this" This style makes Cypress easy to read, easy to write, and easy to teach. --- ## Summary ✅ Cypress is ideal for modern front-end testing ✅ Uses plain, readable syntax that flows like a sentence ✅ Runs directly in the browser, no Selenium or WebDriver required ✅ Great developer experience with built-in UI, debugging, and assertions
← Back
Cloud Services
Next →
Setup & Installation

Testing/EndToEnd/Cypress/Basics/Fundamen.title: Setup
Testing/EndToEnd/Cypress/Basics/Fundamen.bodyMd:
# Setup & Installation --- ## Installing Cypress To install Cypress using npm: ```shell npm install cypress --save-dev ``` This adds Cypress to your project's dev dependencies. --- ## Adding Cypress Scripts to package.json You can create named scripts in your package.json to make launching Cypress easier:
Script Name Command Purpose
cypress:open cypress open Launches Cypress GUI for interactive test runs
cypress:run cypress run Runs all tests headlessly from the terminal
Example scripts section: ```json "scripts": { "cypress:open": "cypress open", "cypress:run": "cypress run" } ``` --- ## Launching Cypress To open the visual Cypress test runner (GUI): ```shell npx cypress open ``` To run all tests in headless mode from the CLI: ```shell npx cypress run ``` --- ## Cypress GUI (Test Runner Interface) When you run npx cypress open, you’ll see: ✅ A list of available test specs ✅ Options for choosing a browser (Chrome, Electron, Edge, etc.) ✅ Real-time logs and screenshots ✅ Time-travel feature (hover to see before/after DOM snapshots) ✅ Full command log for every cy action --- ## Summary ✅ Install Cypress with npm ✅ Add custom launch scripts in package.json ✅ Use npx cypress open for the GUI ✅ Use npx cypress run for headless test automation ✅ The test runner gives powerful debugging and real-time feedback
← Back
Introduction
Next →
Running Tests & Modes

Tools/Containerization/Git/Basics/Comman.title: Common
Tools/Containerization/Git/Basics/Comman.bodyMd:
## Git Commands --- ### Branching & Switching
Command Purpose
git branch <name> Create a new branch
git checkout <name> Switch to a branch
git checkout -b <name> Create and switch to a new branch
### Notes git switch is the newer, clearer way to switch/create branches. git checkout is older but still common in tutorials and still works. Branch naming: - Prefixes like feature/ are optional. If you skip them, keep names consistent and descriptive. - Prefer lowercase with hyphens (january-2026-updates) to avoid casing issues across systems. #### Example: Create and Switch to a Branch ```shell git checkout -b feature/new-notes git status ``` #### Example: Create and Switch to a Branch ```shell git switch -c january-2026-updates git status ``` --- ### Staging & Committing
Command Purpose
git add . Stage all changes in current directory
git add -A Stage all changes, including deletions
git commit -m "message" Commit with message
#### Example: Stage and Commit Changes ```shell git status git add -A git commit -m "Update notes" ``` --- ### Pushing & Pulling
Command Purpose
git push Push changes to remote
git push --set-upstream origin <branch> Set tracking branch and push
git pull Pull latest changes from remote
#### Example: Push a New Branch ```shell git push --set-upstream origin feature/new-notes ``` --- ### Remote Configuration
Command Purpose
git remote -v Show current remotes
git remote set-url origin <url> Change remote origin URL
git fetch --all --prune Update remote branch list and remove stale remote-tracking branches
### Example: Verify Remote URL ```shell git remote set-url origin git remote -v ``` --- ### Note Git commands behave the same across shells. Differences you may encounter usually come from the terminal itself. On Windows, Git Bash is commonly used for Git workflows because it supports Unix-style commands shown in many examples.
← Back
Section: Git Workflow → Nuances
Next →
Commands Status/Diff/Log

Tools/Containerization/Git/Basics/Comman.title: StatusDiffLog
Tools/Containerization/Git/Basics/Comman.bodyMd:
## Status / Diff / Log --- ### Checking Your Working Tree
Command Purpose
git status Show modified, staged, deleted, and untracked files
git status -sb Short status with branch information
--- ### Viewing Changes (Diff)
Command Purpose
git diff Show unstaged changes
git diff --staged Show staged changes
git diff --name-only List changed file names only
git diff --name-status Show file changes with status (M, A, D, R)
--- ### Viewing Commit History (Log)
Command Purpose
git log Show commit history
git log --oneline Condensed one-line commit history
git log --oneline --graph --decorate --all Graph view of commits, branches, and tags
--- ### Renaming Files and Folders (git mv)
Command Purpose
git mv <old-path> <new-path> Rename or move a tracked file or folder
git mv src/Pages src/__tmp_pages__ Windows case-only folder rename (step 1: temporary folder name)
git mv src/__tmp_pages__ src/pages Windows case-only folder rename (step 2: final folder casing)
git mv src/Foo.tsx src/__tmp_foo__.tsx Windows case-only file rename (step 1: temporary file name)
git mv src/__tmp_foo__.tsx src/foo.tsx Windows case-only file rename (step 2: final file casing)
#### Examples of File Renaming Rename a file and verify Git sees it. ```shell git mv src/OldName.tsx src/NewName.tsx git status git diff --name-status ``` Case-only rename on Windows (folder). Use a temporary name so it shows up in PRs. ```shell git mv src/Pages src/__tmp_pages__ git mv src/__tmp_pages__ src/pages git status ``` Case-only rename on Windows (file). ```shell git mv src/Foo.tsx src/__tmp_foo__.tsx git mv src/__tmp_foo__.tsx src/foo.tsx git status ``` --- ### Verifying Renames
Command Purpose
git status Verify Git sees the rename
git diff --name-status Confirm rename shows as R instead of delete/add
--- ### Notes On Windows, case-only renames (such as Pages → pages) may not be detected reliably. Use a temporary rename with git mv to force Git to record the change so it appears correctly in pull requests. Shell note: Git commands (git mv, git status, git diff) work the same in Git Bash and PowerShell. Differences usually come from shell helper commands: - Git Bash uses Unix-style tools (ls, rm, grep) - PowerShell uses different built-in commands and aliases When following shell-based examples on Windows, Git Bash avoids most command differences.
Back: Commands → Common

Tools/Containerization/Git/Basics/Workfl.title: FullWorkflow
Tools/Containerization/Git/Basics/Workfl.bodyMd:
## Full Git Workflow --- ### What is a Workflow? A workflow in Git refers to the process developers follow to make and manage changes in a codebase. It helps maintain clean, organized, and trackable development practices. --- ### Key Concepts in a Workflow - Work is done on a feature branch rather than main. - Changes are staged before they are committed. - Commits represent self-contained, meaningful units of change. - Remote repositories (like GitHub) are used for collaboration and backup. --- ### Branching Basics - Create and switch to a new branch: ```shell git checkout -b branch-name ``` - Just create a branch (without switching): ```shell git branch branch-name ``` NOTE: You would just create a name for your branch with either dashes in between, camel case, or other casing but no spaces where it says branch-name. Examples of valid and invalid branch names: ### ✅ Valid ```shell git checkout -b fix-login-bug git checkout -b feature/ReactNativeSupport git checkout -b hotfix_v2_patch ``` ### ❌ Invalid (don’t do this) ```shell git checkout -b "my broken branch" # Spaces = trouble ``` - Check your current branch: ```shell git status ``` Working on a branch helps keep features or fixes isolated from the main codebase. --- ### Step-by-Step: From Local to Pull Request 1. Make changes to files locally. 2. Stage your changes: ```shell git add . # Stages changes in current directory ``` ```shell git add -A # Stages all changes, including deletions ``` 3. Commit with a message: ```shell git commit -m "your message" ``` 4. Push your changes: ```shell git push ``` - First push of a new branch: ```shell git push --set-upstream origin branch-name ``` --- ### Submitting a Pull Request (PR) - Go to GitHub and open a PR from your branch into main. - After review, click Merge Pull Request. --- ### After Merge Cleanup - Switch back to main: ```shell git checkout main ``` - Pull the latest updates: ```shell git pull ```
← Back
Install/Config
Next →
Nuances

Tools/Containerization/Git/Basics/Workfl.title: Nuances
Tools/Containerization/Git/Basics/Workfl.bodyMd:
## Push / Pull Nuances --- ### Tracking Remotes Every time you push to or pull from a repository, Git communicates with a remote — often GitHub or GitLab — using a configured URL. To check what remote URL your local repository is tracking: ```shell git remote -v ``` This shows the current fetch and push URLs for the remote named origin. --- ### Updating a Remote URL If your repository name or username changes (e.g., on GitHub), you'll need to update the remote URL to continue pushing or pulling. ```shell git remote set-url origin git@github.com:yourUsername/newRepoName.git ``` This updates both fetch and push targets so you can continue syncing with the correct repository. --- ### Push Errors & First-Time Pushes If you try to push a new branch and Git doesn't know which remote branch to track, you'll get an error like: ```shell fatal: The current branch X has no upstream branch. ``` To resolve this, use: ```shell git push --set-upstream origin branch-name ``` This sets up a tracking relationship between your local and remote branch. Future pushes can then be done with just git push. --- ### Pull Conflicts & Out-of-Sync Issues Pulling from the remote can fail if your local branch has changes that conflict with the remote version. Symptoms may include: - ❌ Merge conflicts during git pull - ❌ Rejected updates due to history divergence To handle this: - If your local commits are okay to override, use: ```shell git pull --rebase ``` - If there's a true conflict, Git will pause and ask you to resolve it manually, then commit or continue the rebase. --- ### git add . vs git add -A This is one of the most misunderstood staging behaviors in Git.
Command What It Does
git add . Stages new and modified files in the current directory and its subdirectories.
Does not stage deleted files or changes outside the current directory.
git add -A Stages everything: new, modified, and deleted files across the entire working tree.
--- ### When to Use Which? Use git add . if you only want to add updates in your current working folder. Use git add -A if you want a full snapshot, including file deletions and renames. --- ### Visual Scenario You delete a file from a sibling folder and run: ```shell git add . git commit -m "Removed old utils.js" ``` ❌ The deleted file is not staged — so it remains in the repo! Now try: ```shell git add -A git commit -m "Removed old utils.js" ``` This time, the file deletion is staged and recorded correctly in the commit. --- By understanding these differences and failure points, you’ll avoid common frustrations when collaborating or syncing with a remote.
← Back

## Bootstrap Alerts --- ### What are Bootstrap Alerts? --- Bootstrap provides **pre-styled alert components** for **notifications, warnings, and messages**. - **Easily customizable** using utility classes. - **Supports dismissible alerts** with JavaScript. - Uses **color-coded classes** for **different alert types**. --- ### **Bootstrap Alert Classes** ---
Class Color Usage
.alert-primary Blue For **important messages**
.alert-success Green For **successful actions**
.alert-warning Yellow/Orange For **cautionary messages**
.alert-danger Red For **errors or critical issues**
.alert-info Light Blue For **informational messages**
--- ### **Example Alert** --- ```html
This is a warning alert!
``` ✅ This creates a **yellow/orange** alert for **warnings**. --- ### **Dismissible Alerts** --- You can add a **close button** to dismiss alerts. ```html
Error: Something went wrong!
``` ✅ Adding **.alert-dismissible** makes the alert **closable** with a button. --- ### **Key Takeaways** --- ✅ **Alerts use .alert-* classes** for different **message types**. ✅ **Alerts can be dismissible** using **.alert-dismissible**. ✅ **Each alert type has a distinct color** (e.g., success = green, danger = red).

FrontEnd/CSS/Tools/Frameworks/Bootstrap/.title: Introduction
FrontEnd/CSS/Tools/Frameworks/Bootstrap/.bodyMd:
## Introduction to Bootstrap --- ### What is Bootstrap? Bootstrap is a **CSS and JavaScript framework** designed to make web development **faster and easier**. It provides **pre-written styles, responsive layouts, and UI components**, allowing developers to build professional-looking websites with minimal effort. --- ### Why Use Bootstrap? ✅ Pre-styled Components → Includes buttons, modals, forms, and more. ✅ Responsive Design → Adapts to all screen sizes automatically. ✅ Grid System → Helps create structured layouts easily. ✅ CSS & JavaScript Utilities → Predefined spacing, colors, and typography. ✅ Industry Standard → Used by millions of developers worldwide. ✅ CDN Available → No need for installation; just include a link. --- ### When NOT to Use Bootstrap - If your project requires highly customized styles, Bootstrap may introduce unwanted extra styles. - For small-scale websites, writing custom CSS might be simpler and more efficient. - If you need maximum performance, a lightweight CSS framework or utility-first approach (like Tailwind CSS) may be better. --- ### Adding Bootstrap to Your Project To use Bootstrap, you need to include its CSS and JavaScript files. #### 1. Go to Bootstrap’s official website 📌 [https://getbootstrap.com](https://getbootstrap.com) #### 2. Copy the Bootstrap CDN links On the main page, find the CDN (Content Delivery Network) links. #### 3. Paste them inside your HTML file Add these links inside the head for CSS, and before the closing body tag for JavaScript. ```html ``` Now Bootstrap is ready to use! 🚀

FrontEnd/CSS/Tools/Frameworks/Bootstrap/.title: Grid
FrontEnd/CSS/Tools/Frameworks/Bootstrap/.bodyMd:
## Bootstrap Grid System --- ### What is the Bootstrap Grid System? --- The Bootstrap Grid System is a flexible layout system that divides the page into 12 equal columns. It allows you to create responsive and structured layouts for your webpage. - Uses CSS Flexbox for alignment. - Automatically adjusts for different screen sizes. - You can mix and match column sizes for custom layouts. --- ## **Understanding col-8 and col-4** --- Bootstrap’s grid system is based on 12 columns. The numbers in col-* classes represent how many of those 12 columns the element will take up. #### **Example:** ```html
This div takes up 8 columns
This div takes up 4 columns
``` ✅ col-8 takes up approximately **2/3** (66.67%) of the total row width. ✅ col-4 takes up approximately **1/3** (33.33%) of the total row width. ✅ Since **8 + 4 = 12**, these two elements will fit perfectly in a single row. --- ### **Visual Representation of col-8 and col-4** ---
Column Class Grid Space Taken Percentage of Row
col-8 8 out of 12 66.67% (≈ 2/3 of the row)
col-4 4 out of 12 33.33% (≈ 1/3 of the row)
If col-8 and col-4 were used together in a row, the total would be **12 columns**, meaning they would fit **perfectly** in a single line. --- ### **What Happens If You Exceed 12 Columns?** --- If your total column count **exceeds 12**, Bootstrap will force elements to **wrap to the next line**.

#### **Example (Will Wrap to the Next Line)** ```html
This div takes up 8 columns
This div takes up 6 columns
``` 💡 Since 8 + 6 = 14 (which is **greater than 12**), the second div will **move to the next row automatically**.

--- ### **Making It Responsive (col-sm-8, col-md-8, etc.)** --- You can use responsive column classes to change layouts for different screen sizes. #### **Example:** ```html
This div takes up 8 columns on xl & lg screens, 6 on md, and 12 on sm.
This div takes up 4 columns on xl & lg screens, 6 on md, and 12 on sm.
``` 🔹 **On extra-extra-large screens (xxl ≥ 1400px)** → col-xxl-8 takes **8 columns**, col-xxl-4 takes **4 columns** (8+4=12). 🔹 **On extra-large screens (xl ≥ 1200px)** → col-xl-8 takes **8 columns**, col-xl-4 takes **4 columns** (8+4=12). 🔹 **On large screens (lg ≥ 992px)** → col-lg-8 takes **8 columns**, col-lg-4 takes **4 columns** (8+4=12). 🔹 **On medium screens (md ≥ 768px)** → col-md-6 makes both divs take **6 columns each** (6+6=12). 🔹 **On small screens (sm ≥ 576px)** → Both divs take **12 columns each**, stacking on top of each other. --- ### **Screen Size Breakpoints** ---
Screen Size Class Prefix Min Width
Extra Small (xs) col-* (no prefix) <576px
Small (sm) col-sm-* ≥576px
Medium (md) col-md-* ≥768px
Large (lg) col-lg-* ≥992px
Extra Large (xl) col-xl-* ≥1200px
Extra Extra Large (xxl) col-xxl-* ≥1400px
--- ### **Key Takeaways** --- ✅ col-8 → Takes **8 out of 12** columns (**≈ 66.67% = 2/3 of the row width**). ✅ col-4 → Takes **4 out of 12** columns (**≈ 33.33% = 1/3 of the row width**). ✅ **Columns must be inside a .row** for proper alignment. ✅ If **column count exceeds 12**, Bootstrap **forces wrapping** to the next line. ✅ Use **responsive classes (col-md-8, col-sm-12)** to change layouts based on screen size. --- ### **Summary Table** ---
Column Class Grid Space Taken Behavior
col-8 8/12 columns (66.67%) ≈ 2/3 of the row Stays on the same row if total ≤ 12
col-4 4/12 columns (33.33%) ≈ 1/3 of row Stays on the same row if total ≤ 12
← Back

# Bootstrap Accordions
## Overview
Bootstrap accordions are collapsible UI panels powered by the Collapse plugin. They let users toggle visibility of content sections.
## Basic Accordion Example
```html

This is the first item's accordion body.
``` Use data-bs-parent to ensure only one item stays open at a time. To allow multiple items to be open, omit data-bs-parent.
## Variants
  • Add .accordion-flush for a borderless version.
  • Supports dynamic toggling, collapsing, and accessibility features out of the box.
## Summary
  • Use .accordion, .accordion-item, and .accordion-button for structure.
  • Toggle panels using data-bs-toggle and data-bs-target.
  • Highly useful for collapsible content like FAQs or sidebars.

# Bootstrap Dropdowns
## Overview
Dropdowns are toggleable lists that let users choose from multiple actions or links.
## Basic Dropdown Example
```html
  • Action
  • Another action
  • Something else here
``` Use data-bs-toggle="dropdown" to trigger the menu.
## Extras
  • Align menus right using .dropdown-menu-end.
  • Add .dropdown-divider or .dropdown-header for styling.
  • Use .dropup, .dropstart, or .dropend to change menu direction.
## Summary
  • Use .dropdown, .dropdown-menu, and .dropdown-item for structure.
  • Controlled via the data-bs-toggle attribute.
  • Great for menus, actions, or embedded navigation.

FrontEnd/CSS/Tools/Frameworks/Bootstrap/.title: Modals
FrontEnd/CSS/Tools/Frameworks/Bootstrap/.bodyMd:
# Bootstrap Modals
## What Is a Modal?
A modal is a pop-up window that appears on top of a webpage when triggered, typically by a button. It temporarily disables interaction with the rest of the website (which is usually grayed out) until the user closes the modal or interacts with its content. Common uses for modals:
  • Displaying alerts, confirmations, or additional details.
  • Collecting user input in forms.
  • Showing login prompts, images, or embedded media.
## Basic Modal Structure
A modal consists of several key elements that define its behavior and appearance. ```html
Modal Title
This is the modal body content.
``` Clicking the Open Modal button triggers the modal. Clicking Close or outside the modal dismisses it.
## Key Bootstrap Modal Classes
1. **modal**
  • The base class for all Bootstrap modals.
  • Must be used with modal-dialog and modal-content for proper structure.
2. **modal-dialog**
  • Defines the modal's positioning and size.
  • Can be customized using modal-lg for a large modal or modal-sm for a small modal.
3. **modal-content**
  • Wrapper for all modal content — header, body, and footer.
  • Ensures proper padding and alignment inside the modal.
4. **modal-header**
  • Container for the modal title and close button.
  • Often contains modal-title and a Close button.
5. **modal-title**
  • Defines the modal's title.
  • Usually inside modal-header.
6. **modal-body**
  • Holds the main content of the modal.
  • Can include text, images, forms, or other UI elements.
7. **modal-footer**
  • Typically contains action buttons such as Close or Submit.
  • Helps organize buttons at the bottom of the modal.
## Bootstrap Modal Attributes
8. **data-bs-toggle="modal"**
  • Tells Bootstrap which element should trigger the modal.
  • Replaced data-toggle="modal" in Bootstrap 5.
9. **data-bs-target**
  • Specifies which modal to open when clicking a trigger element.
  • Example: data-bs-target="#exampleModal" opens the modal with id="exampleModal".
  • Replaced data-target="#exampleModal" in Bootstrap 5.
10. **modal fade**
  • Adds a fade-in animation when opening the modal.
  • Use class="modal fade" to enable smooth transitions.
11. **tabindex="-1"**
  • Prevents keyboard focus from interacting with background elements while the modal is open.
  • Ensures accessibility and proper keyboard navigation.
12. **class="close"**
  • Used for the close button inside the modal.
  • Usually placed in modal-header.
  • Replaced by btn-close in Bootstrap 5.
13. **data-bs-dismiss="modal"**
  • Closes the modal when clicking an element with this attribute.
  • Replaced data-dismiss="modal" in Bootstrap 5.
```html ```
## Bootstrap 4 vs Bootstrap 5 Modal Changes
Feature Bootstrap 4
Trigger Attribute data-toggle="modal"
Target Attribute data-target="#modalID"
Dismiss Attribute data-dismiss="modal"
Close Button Class class="close"
Feature Bootstrap 5
Trigger Attribute data-bs-toggle="modal"
Target Attribute data-bs-target="#modalID"
Dismiss Attribute data-bs-dismiss="modal"
Close Button Class class="btn-close"
