## Overview
Tooltips provide extra context when hovering or focusing on an element.
## Basic Usage
```html ``` Tooltips must be initialized via JavaScript. They require Bootstrap's JS bundle which includes Popper.js.
## Options
  • Control placement using data-bs-placement="top" — also accepts bottom, left, and right.
  • Trigger manually or automatically.
  • Add delay, HTML content, or animations via JavaScript options.
## Summary
  • Tooltips are powered by Popper and must be explicitly initialized.
  • Add title and data-bs-toggle to your elements.
  • Use for accessible UI hints, buttons, links, and interactive elements.

FrontEnd/CSS/Tools/Frameworks/Bootstrap/.title: Typography
FrontEnd/CSS/Tools/Frameworks/Bootstrap/.bodyMd:
## Bootstrap Typography --- ### What is Bootstrap Typography? --- Bootstrap provides **predefined typography classes** to control **font size, text style, and emphasis**. - Helps maintain **consistent text styling** across a project. - Uses **utility classes** for **quick customization**. - Supports **responsive font scaling** with .fs-* classes. --- ### **Font Size (.fs-*) Classes** --- Bootstrap provides **six font size classes** to control text size.
Class Size Description
.fs-1 Largest Extra-large font (≈ 2.5rem / 40px)
.fs-2 Larger Large font (≈ 2rem / 32px)
.fs-3 Medium-Large Slightly smaller (≈ 1.75rem / 28px)
.fs-4 Medium Standard text size (≈ 1.5rem / 24px)
.fs-5 Small Smaller text (≈ 1.25rem / 20px)
.fs-6 Smallest Smallest text (≈ 1rem / 16px)
--- ### **Small Text (.small) Class** --- - Makes text **smaller than the surrounding text**. - Often used for **disclaimers, legal text, or captions**. #### **Example:** ```html

This is normal text. This text is smaller.

``` --- ### **Muted Text (.text-muted)** --- - Reduces **text opacity** for **less emphasis**. - Commonly used for **subtle descriptions or secondary content**. #### **Example:** html

This is normal text. This text is muted.

✅ The **.text-muted** class applies **a gray color** to de-emphasize text. --- ### **Key Takeaways** --- ✅ **.fs-* classes** adjust **font sizes** (1 = largest, 6 = smallest). ✅ **.small makes text smaller** than the default size. ✅ **.text-muted de-emphasizes text** by reducing contrast.
← Back
Section: Bootstrap → Utilities → Buttons
Next →
Bootstrap → Typography & Content → Images & Figures

FrontEnd/CSS/Tools/Frameworks/Bootstrap/.title: ColorsAndDisplay
FrontEnd/CSS/Tools/Frameworks/Bootstrap/.bodyMd:
# Bootstrap Colors, Display & Data Attributes
## Bootstrap Color Classes
Bootstrap provides predefined color classes for text, backgrounds, and borders.
  • Primary — typically represents blue.
  • Success — typically represents green.
  • Danger — typically represents red.
  • Warning — typically represents yellow or orange.
  • Info — typically represents light blue.
  • Dark — typically represents dark gray or black.
  • Light — typically represents light gray or white.
Text Class Color
text-primary Blue
text-success Green
text-danger Red
text-warning Yellow / Orange
text-info Light Blue
text-dark Dark Gray / Black
text-light Light Gray / White
Background Class Color
bg-primary Blue Background
bg-success Green Background
bg-danger Red Background
bg-warning Yellow / Orange Background
bg-info Light Blue Background
bg-dark Dark Gray / Black Background
bg-light Light Gray / White Background
## Bootstrap Utilities Overview
Bootstrap provides built-in utility classes that control spacing, colors, typography, and more. To find them go to the Bootstrap documentation and click the Utilities dropdown.
  • Colors — lists available text and background colors.
  • Spacing — predefined padding and margin classes.
  • Typography — controls font styles and sizes.
## Bootstrap Collapse Utility
The collapse class is used to show and hide content dynamically without JavaScript.
  • Allows toggling content visibility with a button or link.
  • Requires data-bs-toggle="collapse" for functionality.
  • Uses id to link the trigger element to the collapsible content.
### Basic Collapse Example
```html
This content will appear when the button is clicked.
``` Clicking the button toggles the visibility of the div.
### Making a Section Expand by Default
To have content visible by default, add show inside the collapse class: ```html
This content is visible by default.
``` The show class makes the section expanded on page load.
### Using Collapse with Multiple Elements
```html
Content 1
Content 2
``` This toggles multiple sections at the same time.
## Bootstrap data-toggle Attribute (Deprecated)
The data-toggle attribute was used in Bootstrap 4 and earlier to enable JavaScript-based behaviors without custom JavaScript.
  • Deprecated in Bootstrap 5 — replaced by data-bs-toggle.
  • Still used in Bootstrap 4 and below.
  • The new format ensures consistency with Bootstrap 5's naming conventions.
Old — Bootstrap 4 New — Bootstrap 5
data-toggle="collapse" data-bs-toggle="collapse"
data-toggle="modal" data-bs-toggle="modal"
Example using the correct Bootstrap 5 syntax: ```html
This content will appear when the button is clicked.
``` This is the correct Bootstrap 5 syntax. NOTE: Do not use data-toggle in Bootstrap 5.
## Bootstrap data-target Attribute (Deprecated)
The data-target attribute was used in Bootstrap 4 and earlier to define the element that should be affected by data-toggle.
  • Deprecated in Bootstrap 5 — replaced by data-bs-target.
  • Still used in Bootstrap 4 and below.
  • The new format follows Bootstrap 5's naming convention for consistency.
Old — Bootstrap 4 New — Bootstrap 5
data-target="#exampleCollapse" data-bs-target="#exampleCollapse"
data-target="#myModal" data-bs-target="#myModal"
Example using the correct Bootstrap 5 syntax: ```html
This content will appear when the button is clicked.
``` This is the correct Bootstrap 5 syntax. Do not use data-target in Bootstrap 5.
← Back
Bootstrap → Utilities → Spacing & Sizing
Next →
Bootstrap → Utilities → Buttons

FrontEnd/CSS/Tools/Frameworks/Bootstrap/.title: SpacingAndSizing
FrontEnd/CSS/Tools/Frameworks/Bootstrap/.bodyMd:
# Bootstrap Spacing Utilities
## Spacing Class Structure
Bootstrap provides predefined classes for padding and margins using the pattern: {property}{sides}-{size}
  • p — Padding
  • m — Margin
  • x — Left and Right
  • y — Top and Bottom
  • t — Top
  • b — Bottom
  • l — Left
  • r — Right
  • size — A number from 0 to 5 that determines spacing
## Bootstrap Spacing Scale
Size Pixels
0 0px
1 4px
2 8px
3 16px
4 24px
5 48px
Size rem
0 0rem
1 0.25rem
2 0.5rem
3 1rem
4 1.5rem
5 3rem
## Examples of Bootstrap Spacing Classes
1. **Padding on All Sides**
  • p-5 — applies 48px (3rem) padding on all sides.
2. **Padding on the Y-Axis (Top & Bottom)**
  • py-5 — applies 48px padding on the top and bottom.
3. **Padding on the X-Axis (Left & Right)**
  • px-5 — applies 48px padding on the left and right.
4. **Margin on All Sides**
  • m-3 — applies 16px margin on all sides.
## Summary of Bootstrap Spacing Classes
Class Meaning
p-5 Padding on all sides (48px)
py-5 Padding on top and bottom (48px)
Class Example
p-5 <div class="p-5">...</div>
py-5 <div class="py-5">...</div>
← Back

Next →

FrontEnd/CSS/Tools/Preprocessors/Sass/Ad.title: ExtendAndPlaceholders
FrontEnd/CSS/Tools/Preprocessors/Sass/Ad.bodyMd:
## **Sass Extend & Placeholder Selectors** --- ### **What is @extend in Sass?** --- The @extend directive allows elements to **share styles without duplicating code**. - It **reduces CSS repetition** by merging selectors. - Useful for **inheritance-based styling** (e.g., buttons, alerts, common utilities). ✅ **Example of @extend:** ```scss .button background-color: blue color: white padding: 10px .button-primary @extend .button ``` ✅ **CSS Output:** ```scss .button, .button-primary background-color: blue color: white padding: 10px ``` --- ### **Benefits of @extend** --- ✅ Reduces **duplicate styles** in compiled CSS. ✅ Keeps styles **organized** with shared rules. ✅ Ensures **efficient inheritance** for utility classes. --- ### **What are Placeholder Selectors (%placeholder)?** --- Placeholders (%) work **like @extend** but **do not appear in the final CSS** unless used. #### **Why Use %placeholder Instead of @extend?** - **Prevents unused styles** from appearing in CSS. - **Keeps CSS clean**—only added when extended. ✅ **Example:** ```scss %button-styles background-color: blue color: white padding: 10px .button-primary @extend %button-styles ``` ✅ **CSS Output (Only When Used):** ```scss .button-primary background-color: blue color: white padding: 10px ``` ✅ **Unlike @extend, placeholders (%) do not generate unused CSS.** --- ### **Comparing @extend vs. Mixins** ---
Feature @extend @mixin
Reduces Duplicate CSS? ✅ Yes ❌ No (generates new CSS each time)
Allows Parameters? ❌ No ✅ Yes
Best For Reusing styles with inheritance Reusable components with dynamic values
✅ **Use @extend for shared styles and @mixin for reusable components.** --- ### **Best Practices for Using @extend** --- ✅ **Use placeholders (%) when possible** to avoid generating extra unused styles. ✅ **Avoid overextending selectors**, as it can lead to **deeply nested CSS**. ✅ **Use @mixin when variables or arguments are needed** instead of @extend. --- ### **Common Mistakes with @extend** --- ❌ **Overusing @extend inside deep structures** ```scss .nav @extend .container ``` ❌ **Extending classes that change frequently** ```scss .box @extend .old-box-style /* Risky if .old-box-style changes later */ ``` ✅ **Correct Usage:** ```scss %shared-button background: blue color: white .button-primary @extend %shared-button ``` ✅ **Placeholders prevent unnecessary styles from appearing in the final CSS.** --- ### **Conclusion** --- - **@extend allows elements to inherit styles without duplication.** - **Placeholders (%) prevent unused CSS from appearing in the final stylesheet.** - **Use @mixin instead of @extend when variables are needed.** ✅ **Now you understand how to use @extend & Placeholder Selectors in Sass!** 🚀
← Back

FrontEnd/CSS/Tools/Preprocessors/Sass/Ad.title: OperatorsAndCalculations
FrontEnd/CSS/Tools/Preprocessors/Sass/Ad.bodyMd:
## **Sass Operators & Calculations** --- ### **What Are Operators in Sass?** --- Operators allow mathematical and logical calculations inside Sass. - They make dynamic styling easier by allowing **calculations, conditions, and logic.** - Operators work with **numbers, colors, strings, and boolean values.** ✅ **Types of Operators in Sass:**
Type Operators Example
Arithmetic +, -, *, /, % width: 100% / 3
Comparison ==, !=, <, >, <=, >= @if $size > 10px
Boolean (Logic) and, or, not @if ($dark-theme and $enabled)
--- ### **1️⃣ Arithmetic Operators in Sass** --- Arithmetic operators allow **basic math operations** inside styles. #### **Example: Multiplication & Division** ```scss $base-size: 10px .box width: $base-size * 2 /* 20px */ margin: $base-size / 2 /* 5px */ ``` ✅ **Useful for setting dynamic widths, padding, and margins.** --- ### **2️⃣ Operators in Color Functions** --- Operators work with color functions to create **lighter or darker color variations.** #### **Example: Lightening & Darkening Colors** ```scss $primary: blue .button background-color: lighten($primary, 10%) border-color: darken($primary, 5%) ``` ✅ **This allows colors to be dynamically adjusted within styles.** --- ### **3️⃣ Comparison Operators in Conditional Logic** --- Comparison operators **evaluate conditions** in @if statements. #### **Example: Changing Font Size Based on a Variable** ```scss $size: 12px @if $size > 10px .box font-size: $size ``` ✅ **This ensures certain styles only apply when conditions are met.** --- ### **4️⃣ Boolean Operators in Sass** --- Boolean operators (and, or, not) allow **logic-based styling.** #### **Example: Applying Styles Based on Multiple Conditions** ```scss $dark-theme: true $enabled: true @if ($dark-theme and $enabled) body background: black color: white ``` ✅ **This ensures styles are only applied when both conditions are met.** --- ### **Best Practices for Using Operators in Sass** --- ✅ **Use arithmetic for calculations instead of hardcoding values.** ✅ **Avoid dividing by zero (/ 0 will cause errors).** ✅ **Use comparison operators for responsive design logic (@if).** --- ### **Conclusion** --- - **Operators allow calculations, logic, and conditions in Sass.** - **Arithmetic operators (+, -, *, /, %) simplify calculations.** - **Comparison and boolean operators make styles dynamic.**
← Back

FrontEnd/CSS/Tools/Preprocessors/Sass/Ba.title: Introduction
FrontEnd/CSS/Tools/Preprocessors/Sass/Ba.bodyMd:
# What Is Sass?
## Overview
Sass (Syntactically Awesome Stylesheets) is a CSS extension language that adds extra functionality to CSS.
  • Introduces variables, nesting, mixins, and logic-based styling.
  • Helps reduce repetition in stylesheets.
  • Compiles into standard CSS, making it browser-compatible.
  • Used by popular frameworks like Bootstrap.
  • Browsers cannot read Sass directly — it must be compiled first.
## Sass vs. CSS
Sass extends CSS with additional features not available in standard CSS.
Aspect Sass
Standardized? No — Sass is not an official W3C standard.
Works in Browsers? No — browsers do not understand Sass directly.
Compilation Needed? Yes — Sass must be compiled into CSS.
Advanced Features? Yes — includes variables, mixins, loops, and more.
Aspect CSS
Standardized? Yes — CSS is an official W3C standard.
Works in Browsers? Yes — CSS works natively in browsers.
Compilation Needed? No — CSS does not require compilation.
Advanced Features? No — CSS is simpler with fewer dynamic features.
Sass is not a replacement for CSS — it enhances it.
## What Is W3C?
W3C (World Wide Web Consortium) is the main international organization that develops web standards, including CSS, HTML, and JavaScript.
  • It ensures consistency across browsers and devices.
  • CSS is an official W3C standard — Sass is not.
  • Sass is a preprocessor that adds features on top of CSS but is not part of the official web standards.
  • Browsers support W3C standards natively but do not support Sass directly.
## Sass File Extensions
Sass uses two different file extensions:
Extension Description
.scss The most common syntax — similar to CSS with curly braces and semicolons.
.sass A shorthand syntax that removes curly braces and semicolons, relying on indentation instead.
SCSS is preferred because it is easier to read and write, especially for developers already familiar with CSS.
## How Sass Works
1. **Write styles in a Sass file**
  • Use either .scss or .sass syntax.
2. **Compile Sass into standard CSS**
  • Use a preprocessor tool to convert Sass into browser-readable CSS.
3. **Link the compiled file to your HTML**
  • Link the compiled .css file to your HTML page as you normally would.
Example Sass file (.scss): ```css $primary-color: blue; button { background-color: $primary-color; color: white; padding: 10px; } ``` After compilation it becomes standard CSS: ```css button { background-color: blue; color: white; padding: 10px; } ``` Sass variables make code reusable and easier to maintain.
## Compiling Sass
Before using Sass in a project you need to compile your Sass files into standard CSS.
### Installing Sass
```shell npm install sass --save-dev ```
### Compiling a Single File
```shell npx sass Sass/main.sass Sass/main.css ```
  • Sass/main.sass — the input Sass file.
  • Sass/main.css — the output CSS file.
For an entire directory: ```shell npx sass Sass:dist ```
  • Sass/ — folder containing .sass files.
  • dist/ — folder where compiled .css files are saved.
### Watching for Changes
To automatically recompile when changes are made: ```shell npx sass --watch Sass/main.sass Sass/main.css ``` For an entire folder: ```shell npx sass --watch Sass:dist ```
### Adding Compilation to package.json
To automate Sass compilation add scripts to your package.json: ```js { "scripts": { "sass": "sass Sass/main.sass Sass/main.css", "sass:watch": "sass --watch Sass/main.sass Sass/main.css", "sass:watch-all": "sass --watch Sass:dist" } } ``` Then run them from the CLI: ```shell npm run sass ``` ```shell npm run sass:watch ``` ```shell npm run sass:watch-all ```
### Identifying Watched Files
When using --watch, Sass provides real-time feedback in the terminal. When watching a single file you will see: ```shell Sass is watching for changes... Compiled Sass/main.sass to Sass/main.css. ``` If you modify the file it automatically recompiles: ```shell Change detected. Recompiling... Compiled Sass/main.sass to Sass/main.css. ``` When watching multiple files: ```shell Sass is watching for changes... Watching Sass/*.sass ``` If any file inside the folder changes: ```shell Change detected in Sass/header.sass. Recompiling... Compiled Sass/header.sass to dist/header.css. ```

FrontEnd/CSS/Tools/Preprocessors/Sass/Ba.title: MixinsAndFunctions
FrontEnd/CSS/Tools/Preprocessors/Sass/Ba.bodyMd:
# Mixins & Functions in Sass
## What Are Mixins?
Mixins allow you to reuse groups of styles without repeating code.
  • They accept parameters for dynamic styling.
  • Unlike @extend, mixins do not merge selectors, preventing unintended styles.
  • They are useful for responsive design, utility styles, and vendor prefixes.
Example of a mixin: ```css @mixin button-styles($bgColor) background-color: $bgColor color: white padding: 10px .button @include button-styles(blue) ```
## Creating and Using Mixins
### Basic Mixin Syntax
```css @mixin mixin-name(parameters) property: value @include mixin-name(arguments) ```
### Example: Mixin for Buttons
```css @mixin button-styles($bgColor, $textColor: white) background-color: $bgColor color: $textColor padding: 10px .button-primary @include button-styles(blue) .button-danger @include button-styles(red, black) ``` Mixins allow reusable styles with dynamic values.
## Mixin vs. @extend
Both @mixin and @extend allow code reuse but they work differently.
Feature @mixin
Allows Parameters? Yes
Repeats Code in CSS? Yes
Best For Reusable styles with variables
Feature @extend
Allows Parameters? No
Repeats Code in CSS? No — merges selectors
Best For Inheritance-based styles
Use @mixin for dynamic styles and @extend for inheritance.
## Best Practices for Using Mixins
  • Keep mixins small and modular — avoid bloated CSS.
  • Use mixins for reusable components such as buttons, grids, and breakpoints.
  • Avoid excessive mixins — use @extend when applicable.
## Using Mixins for Responsive Design
Mixins are useful for media queries and responsive layouts. ```css @mixin mobile @media screen and (max-width: 600px) @content .container width: 80% @include mobile width: 100% ``` @content inside mixins allows dynamic blocks of CSS.
## Sass Functions
Functions in Sass allow you to process values and return a result. ```css @function calculate-padding($size) @return $size * 2 .box padding: calculate-padding(10px) ``` Use functions for calculations, color manipulation, or custom logic.
## Summary
  • Mixins allow reusable styles with dynamic values.
  • Use @extend for shared styles and @mixin for dynamic styles.
  • Functions process values and return results for calculations.
← Back

Section: Sass → Syntax → SCSS vs Sass Syntax

FrontEnd/CSS/Tools/Preprocessors/Sass/Ba.title: VariablesNesting
FrontEnd/CSS/Tools/Preprocessors/Sass/Ba.bodyMd:
# Variables & Nesting in Sass
## Sass Variables
Sass allows the use of variables to store reusable values, making styles easier to maintain and update.
### Variable Syntax
```css $variableName: value; ```
  • Must start with $ — for example: $primaryColor.
  • Must include a : (colon) after the variable name.
  • Variables can use camelCase, kebab-case, or snake_case.
  • In indented Sass syntax, no curly braces or semicolons are required — only whitespace matters.
### Variable Examples
```css $primaryColor: blue; $fontSize: 16px; $borderRadius: 5px; .button { background-color: $primaryColor; font-size: $fontSize; border-radius: $borderRadius; } ``` Variables make styles reusable and easier to maintain across a project.
## Nesting
Sass allows nesting selectors inside other selectors to keep styles more organized and reflect HTML structure. ```css .nav { background: black; .nav-item { color: white; } } ``` Nesting helps structure styles but should not go too deep — excessive nesting makes stylesheets harder to maintain.
## Whitespace & Indentation
In the indented .sass syntax, Sass relies on indentation instead of curly braces and semicolons. Correct indentation is required. Incorrect — missing indentation: ```css .button background-color: blue color: white ``` Correct — proper indentation: ```css .button background-color: blue color: white ```
## Summary
  • Sass variables store reusable values using $ and :.
  • Nesting allows selectors to be structured inside parent elements for better readability.
  • In indented Sass syntax, strict indentation is required since curly braces and semicolons are not used.
← Back
Sass → Fundamentals → Introduction
Next →
Sass → Fundamentals → Mixins & Functions

FrontEnd/CSS/Tools/Preprocessors/Sass/Ba.title: PartialsImports
FrontEnd/CSS/Tools/Preprocessors/Sass/Ba.bodyMd:
# Partials & Imports in Sass
## What Are Partials?
Partials are smaller Sass files that can be imported into other Sass files.
  • Partials help organize styles into modular files.
  • They are not compiled into CSS directly unless imported.
  • A partial file starts with an underscore (_) to indicate it should be included in another file.
Example partial file (_buttons.sass): ```css $buttonColor: blue .button background-color: $buttonColor color: white padding: 10px ```
## Using @import to Include Partials
Sass provides the @import directive to include partials inside other Sass files. ```css @import "filename" ```
  • Do not include the underscore (_) or file extension (.sass) when importing.
  • Sass automatically looks for a file named _filename.sass.
Example importing _buttons.sass into styles.sass: ```css @import "buttons" body font-family: Arial, sans-serif ```
## Why @import Is Deprecated
In modern Sass, @import is deprecated in favor of @use and @forward. Problems with @import:
  • Causes global namespace pollution — mixes all styles together.
  • Files are imported multiple times, leading to duplicate styles.
  • No clear dependency management between files.
The solution:
  • @use is the new way to import files while keeping styles modular.
  • @forward is used in shared modules to expose styles to multiple files.
## @import vs. @use
Feature @import
Global Namespace Yes — all variables and mixins are global.
Duplicate Imports Yes — imports the same file multiple times.
Recommended? No — deprecated in newer Sass versions.
Feature @use
Global Namespace No — keeps variables private by default.
Duplicate Imports No — each file is only loaded once.
Recommended? Yes — use @use instead of @import.
## Replacing @import with @use
Instead of @import, use @use to import partials in a modular way. Old approach using @import: ```css @import "buttons" ``` New approach using @use: ```css @use "buttons" ```
## Using @forward for Shared Modules
If you have a central Sass file that imports multiple files, use @forward to expose styles. Example using @forward in _all.sass: ```css @forward "buttons" @forward "typography" ``` Then in styles.sass you can import everything at once: ```css @use "all" ```
## Best Practices
  • Use @use instead of @import in modern Sass.
  • Keep files modular — one purpose per partial.
  • Use @forward to group shared files.
  • Follow naming conventions — prefix partials with _ such as _filename.sass.
## Summary
  • Partials (_filename.sass) help modularize styles.
  • @import is deprecated — use @use and @forward instead.
  • @use prevents global namespace pollution.
  • @forward is useful for sharing styles across multiple files.
